name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنِّى لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢٥)
```

```english
Certainly We sent Noah to his people [to declare]: ‘Indeed I am a manifest warner to you. (25)
```

```arabic
أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢٦)
```

```english
Worship none but Allah. Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a painful day.’ (26)
```

```arabic
فَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ مَا نَرَىٰكَ إِلَّا بَشَرًا مِّثْلَنَا وَمَا نَرَىٰكَ ٱتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ أَرَاذِلُنَا بَادِىَ ٱلرَّأْىِ وَمَا نَرَىٰ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍۭ بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كَـٰذِبِينَ (٢٧)
```

```english
But the elite of the faithless from among his people said, ‘We do not see you to be anything but a human being like ourselves, and we do not see anyone following you except those who are simpleminded riffraff from our midst. Nor do we see that you have any merit over us. Indeed, we consider you to be liars.’ (27)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَءَاتَىٰنِى رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوهَا وَأَنتُمْ لَهَا كَـٰرِهُونَ (٢٨)
```

```english
He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and He has granted me His own mercy—though it should be invisible to you—shall we force it upon you while you are averse to it? (28)
```

```arabic
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ لَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مَالًا ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِطَارِدِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَـٰكِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُمْ قَوْمًا تَجْهَلُونَ (٢٩)
```

```english
O my people! I do not ask you any material reward for it. My reward lies only with Allah. But I will not drive away those who have faith: they will indeed encounter their Lord. But I see that you are an ignorant lot. (29)
```

```arabic
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ مَن يَنصُرُنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِن طَرَدتُّهُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٣٠)
```

```english
O my people! Who would come to my help against Allah were I to drive them away? Will you not then take admonition? (30)
```

```arabic
وَلَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِندِى خَزَآئِنُ ٱللَّهِ وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ وَلَآ أَقُولُ إِنِّى مَلَكٌ وَلَآ أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِىٓ أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَن يُؤْتِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ خَيْرًا ۖ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ إِنِّىٓ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, neither do I know the Unseen. I do not claim to be an angel, neither do I say of those who are despicable in your eyes that Allah will not grant them any good—Allah knows best what is in their hearts—for then I would indeed be a wrongdoer.’ (31)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰنُوحُ قَدْ جَـٰدَلْتَنَا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدَٰلَنَا فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٣٢)
```

```english
They said, ‘O Noah, you have disputed with us already, and you have disputed with us exceedingly. Now bring us what you threaten us with, should you be truthful. (32)
```

```arabic
قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ ٱللَّهُ إِن شَآءَ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ (٣٣)
```

```english
He said, ‘Allah will indeed bring it on you if He wishes, and you cannot frustrate [Him]. (33)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِىٓ إِنْ أَرَدتُّ أَنْ أَنصَحَ لَكُمْ إِن كَانَ ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ ۚ هُوَ رَبُّكُمْ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٣٤)
```

```english
My exhorting will not benefit you, much as I may seek to exhort you, if Allah desires to consign you to perversity. He is your Lord, and to Him you shall be brought back.’ (34)
```

```arabic
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ إِنِ ٱفْتَرَيْتُهُۥ فَعَلَىَّ إِجْرَامِى وَأَنَا۠ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُجْرِمُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Should I have fabricated it, then my guilt will be upon me, and I am absolved of your guilty conduct.’ (35)
```

## (Commentary)

This marks the beginning of the stories of the Prophets (peace be upon them). It commences with Nuh (Noah), followed by a group of those who came after him, such as Hud, Salih, Ibrahim (Abraham), Lut (Lot), Shu’ayb, and Musa (Moses) (peace be upon him). The story of Nuh has been divided into several chapters, the first of which is his (peace be upon him) argumentation with his people regarding monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*. He (peace be upon him) was the first of the Prophets to rise for monotheism against idolatry (الوثنية), according to what Allah the Exalted has mentioned in His Book. Most of what is narrated of his (peace be upon him) argumentation with his people consists of "arguing in a way that is best," some of it consists of exhortation (الموعظة), and a small portion consists of wisdom (الحكمة). This is what suits the initial human reflection and the simple, ancient man, particularly their social thinking, in which nothing appears except the accumulated ideas of average individuals in understanding.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً إِلى قَوْمِهِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ** — "Certainly We sent Noah to his people [and he said]: ‘Indeed I am a manifest warner to you." (11:25). The well-known recitation is **إِنِّي** (Indeed I) with the *kasrah* on the *hamzah*, based on the estimation of a suppressed verb of "saying." It has also been recited as *annī* with the *fathah* on the *hamzah* by removing the preposition, the estimation being "with [the message] that I am a manifest warner to you." The sentence—meaning His saying: **إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ**—is, in any case, a summary explanation of what he was sent with; for everything he conveyed to his people from his Lord and was sent with to them is a manifest warning, thus he is a manifest warner.

Just as if he had said, "What I shall deliver to you of speech is a manifest warning," it would have been an explanation of everything he was sent with to them in the briefest word; similarly, his saying: **إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ** is a summary explanation of that. However, it adds to the former by clarifying the mark (السمة) of his own self: that he is a messenger from Allah to them to warn them of Allah's punishment, and he has no authority beyond being a medium who carries the message.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللهَ إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ** — "that you should worship no one but Allah. Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a painful day.’" (11:26). This is a second explanation of what he was sent with, or an explanation of his saying: **إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ**, and the outcome of both views is the same. The word **أَنْ** (that) is, in any case, explanatory (مفسرة). The meaning is that the sum total of his message is the prohibition of worshipping other than Allah the Exalted through the path of warning and instilling fear.

Some commentators have mentioned that the sentence—meaning His saying: **أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا** etc.—is an appositive (بدل) for His saying: **إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ**, or the object (مفعول) of the word "manifest" (مبين). Perhaps the context supports what we previously presented.

It appears that what is meant by "the punishment of a painful day" is the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) — *the total divine destruction of a people in this world* — rather than the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, or it could encompass both punishments. This is indicated by their saying to him, in what Allah the Exalted will narrate from them: **يا نُوحُ قَدْ جادَلْتَنا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدالَنا فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ قالَ إِنَّما يَأْتِيكُمْ بِهِ اللهُ إِنْ شاءَ** — "They said, ‘O Noah, you have disputed with us and you have disputed with us much. Now bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful.’ He said, ‘Allah will bring it to you only if He wishes...’" (11:32-33). This is clearly referring to the punishment of extirpation.

Thus, he (peace be upon him) was calling them to reject the worship of idols and frightening them of a day in which a painful (أليم)—meaning agonizing—punishment would descend upon them from Allah. The attribution of "painful" to the "day" rather than the "punishment" in His saying: **عَذابَ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ** is a case of describing the container (الظرف) with the attribute of the contained (المظروف).

By what has preceded, that which is sometimes said is refuted: that the punishment of the polytheists is a certainty, not a mere possibility, so what is the reason for his (peace be upon him) fear regarding their certain punishment? Fear is only valid regarding that which is possible to occur, not that which is certain.

In short, he (peace be upon him) was calling them to the monotheism of Allah, the Glorified, by frightening them with the punishment. He only frightened them because they used to worship idols out of fear of their wrath. Nuh (peace be upon him) countered them by stating that Allah, the Glorified, is the One Who created them and managed the affairs of their lives and their livelihood by creating the heavens and the earth, the shining of the sun and the moon, the sending down of rain, the sprouting of the earth, the creation of gardens, and the cleaving of rivers, according to what the Exalted narrates from him (peace be upon him) in Surah Nuh.

Since this was the case, Allah, the Glorified, is their Lord (الرب) — *the Sustainer, Master, and Regulator of all affairs* — and there is no Lord besides Him; so let them fear His punishment and worship Him alone.

This argument is, in reality, a demonstrative argument (حجة برهانية) based on certainty (اليقين). However, they only received it as a dialectical argument (حجة جدلية) based on conjecture (الظن). Due to the simplicity of their understanding, they expected the wrath and punishment of the Lord for disobedience because they viewed Him as a guardian (ولي) of their affairs and a reformer of their condition. Thus, they measured His affair against the affair of human guardians who rule over those beneath them in society, to whose status submission is required and to whose will surrender is mandatory. If those beneath them were too arrogant to submit or surrender to their will, they would become angry with them and punish them for their crimes and rebellion.

Based on this analogy, it is necessary to please the Lord—or the lords—to whom the affair of the cosmos and the guardianship of the existing order return. It is necessary to please Him and extinguish the fire of His anger by submitting to Him and seeking proximity to Him by offering oblations, sacrifices, and other forms of worship. This is what they believed, and it is based on conjecture.

However, the issue of the descent of punishment for refusing to worship Allah the Exalted and for being arrogant toward surrendering and submitting to the presence of Divinity (الربوبية) is a factual, certain matter. For among the universal laws (النواميس الكلية) running through the cosmos is the necessity of the weak submitting to the strong, and the passive, conquered entity submitting to the active, conquering one. So what then of Allah, the One, the All-Conquering (القهار), to Whom is the return of all affairs?

Allah, the Glorified, has originated the parts of the cosmos and linked some to others, then He made events run according to the system of causes (نظام الأسباب). Accordingly, everything runs within the system of its existence. If it deviates from the line drawn for it by the rest of the causes, this leads to a disruption of their system, and that constitutes a conflict between it and them. At that point, the rest of the cosmic causes from the parts of existence rise up to rectify its affair and return it to a line that suits them, thereby repelling evil from themselves. If this part that deviated from its drawn line rectifies itself, then so be it; otherwise, the crushing forces of causes and the descending calamities and trials will shatter it. This, too, is among the universal laws.

Man, who is one of the parts of the cosmos, has a line in his life drawn for him by Fashioning and Origination. If he traverses it, it guides him to his felicity, and he thereby accords with the rest of the parts of the cosmos; the gates of heaven open for him with their blessings, and the earth yields to him its treasures of goodness. This is Islam (الإسلام), which is the religion (الدين) — *the divinely ordained way of life* — with Allah the Exalted, to which the call of Nuh and the Prophets and Messengers (peace be upon them) after him invited.

But if he oversteps it and deviates from it, he has conflicted with the causes of the cosmos and the parts of existence in their running system and has crowded them in the affairs of their lives. Thus, let him expect bitter trial and wait for punishment and distress. If he rectifies his affair and submits to the will of Allah, the Glorified—which is that which shatters him of the general causes—then it is hoped that blessing will be renewed for him after the affliction; otherwise, it is destruction and annihilation. Indeed, Allah is independent of all the worlds (العالمين). This discussion has already preceded in some previous parts of the book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَقالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا** — "The elite of the faithless of his people said, ‘We do not see you to be anything but a human being like ourselves..." (11:27) to the end of the verse. The *fā’* at the beginning of the verse is for branching (تفريع) their response from the speech of Nuh (peace be upon him). In it is an indication that they rushed to him with rejection and denial without reflecting within themselves to choose what was most beneficial for them.

The respondents were the elite (الملأ) of his people—the nobles and the great ones—who disbelieved in him. They did not address in their response the argument for monotheism that he presented to them; rather, they occupied themselves with denying his message and being arrogant toward obeying him. For his saying: **إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ** to the end of the two verses contained the claim of messengership and alluded to the necessity of following him. He stated this explicitly in what is narrated from him elsewhere; the Exalted said: **قالَ يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ وَأَطِيعُونِ** — "He said, ‘O my people! Indeed I am a manifest warner to you, [announcing] that you should worship Allah and be wary of Him, and obey me." (71:2-3).

The sum total of what Allah the Exalted narrated of their response is that there is no evidence for the necessity of following you; rather, the evidence is to the contrary. It is, in reality, two arguments arranged by way of digression and progression (الإضراب والترقي); for this reason, they delayed their saying: **بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كاذِبِينَ** (rather we think you are liars).

The first argument, whose implication is the lack of evidence for the necessity of following him, is explained in three ways: His saying: **ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً** etc., His saying: **وَما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ** etc., and His saying: **وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا** etc.

The argument in all its parts is built upon the denial of what lies beyond the senses (ما وراء الحس), as we shall explain; for this reason, they repeated their saying: "We do not see" (ما نراك) and "We do not see" (نرى).

So His saying: **ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا** is the first of their responses to what Nuh (peace be upon him) claimed of messengership. They clung to similarity (المماثلة), as was the habit of all other nations with their Prophets, according to what Allah the Exalted has narrated in His Book. Its formulation is: "You are like us in humanity, and if you were a messenger to us from Allah, you would not be so. We observe nothing from you except that you are a human being like us; and since you are a human being like us, there is no motive to follow you."

In this speech is a denial of his (peace be upon him) messengership on the grounds that he is nothing but a human being like them, then a conclusion from that that there is no evidence for the necessity of following him. The evidence for what we have mentioned is the saying of Nuh (peace be upon him) in what Allah the Exalted will narrate of his speech: **يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي** etc.

The matter has become confused for some commentators, who formulated their saying: **ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا** as them equating him with themselves in social weight and concluding from it that there is no reason to follow him. He said in the commentary of the verse: "They answered him with four refuted arguments. The first: that he is a human being like them, so they equated him with themselves in general. This indicates that he (peace be upon him) was from their class or close to it in his household and his person, and thus was every messenger from the midst of his people. The point of the response is that equality negates the claim of the superiority of one of the equals over the other by making one a follower-obeyer and the other a followed-obeyed, because it is a preference without a preferrer (ترجيح بغير مرجح)." [End of quote].

If the meaning were as he mentioned, it would have been proper for the speech to be: "You are like us" or "We see you as like us," without saying: "We do not see you to be anything but a human being like ourselves," mentioning that he is a human being when there is no need to point to his humanity. Furthermore, the meaning of the speech would return to the intent of their later saying: "nor do we see you to have any merit over us," and it would be redundant speech.

It is strange that he deduced from the speech his (peace be upon him) equality with them in household and personality, then said: "and thus was every messenger from the midst of his people," while among the messengers are those like Ibrahim, Sulayman (Solomon), and Ayyub (Job) (peace be upon them).

And His saying: **وَما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ** — "...nor do we see that anyone follows you except the lowest of us, [who do so] offhand..." (11:27). It is said in *al-Mufradat*: **الرذل** (the base) with a *fathah* on the *rā’*, and *al-ridhāl* with a *kasrah*, refers to that which is desired against due to its poor quality. The Exalted said: **وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرَدُّ إِلى أَرْذَلِ الْعُمُرِ** — "...and some of you are relegated to the lowest state of life..." (16:70). And He said: **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ**. And He said: **قالُوا أَنُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَاتَّبَعَكَ الْأَرْذَلُونَ** — "They said, ‘Shall we believe in you when it is the lowest who follow you?’" (26:111). It is the plural of *al-ardhal*.

It is said in *al-Majma‘*: **الرذل** is the mean and insignificant of everything. The plural is *ardhal*, then it is pluralized as *arādhil*, like your saying: *kalb* (dog), *aklub*, and *akālib*. It is also possible that it is the plural of *al-ardhal*, so it would be like *akābir* (the greatest) as the plural of *akbar*.

He said: And **الرأي** (opinion/sight) is seeing, from His saying: **يَرَوْنَهُمْ مِثْلَيْهِمْ رأي الْعَيْنِ** — "...seeing them with their own eyes to be twice as many as they were..." (3:13), meaning the seeing of the eye. *Al-ra’y* is also what a person sees [as an opinion] in a matter, and its plural is *ārā’*. [End of quote].

It is said in *al-Mufradat*: And His saying: **بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ** means that which begins of the opinion, which is the unripened opinion. It has been recited as **بادي** without a *hamzah*, meaning that which appears of the opinion without having been reflected upon. [End of quote].

His saying: **بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ** could possibly be a qualifier for His saying: **هُمْ أَراذِلُنا**, meaning their being the lowest and the base among us is known in the apparent opinion and view, or at first glance.

It could also be a qualifier for His saying: **اتَّبَعَكَ**, meaning they followed you in the apparent opinion or at the beginning of it without depth or reflection; had they reflected a little and turned your affair over, they would not have followed you. This possibility cannot do without the repetition of the verb a second time, the estimation being: "they followed you at the beginning of the matter," otherwise the meaning would be disrupted if it were not repeated and it were said: "We do not see that anyone follows you in the beginning of the opinion except those who are the lowest of us." In short, the meaning of the verse is: "We observe that your followers are the lowest and the meanest of the people. If we were to follow you, we would be equal to them and enter their ranks, and this contradicts our nobility and lowers our status in society." In the speech is an allusion to the falsehood of his (peace be upon him) messengership by way of necessary implication (دلالة الالتزام); for it is among the beliefs of the common folk that if a saying were true and beneficial, the nobles, the great ones, and the possessors of power and wealth would have followed it. If they refrain from it, or if the meanest and the weakest follow it—such as slaves, the indigent, and the poor who have no share of wealth or prestige and no status among the common folk—then there is no good in it.

And His saying: **وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا مِنْ فَضْلٍ** — "...nor do we see you to have any merit over us..." (11:27). What is meant is the negation of absolute merit, whether of worldly enjoyment that they are exclusively blessed with, or something of the matters of the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — such as knowledge of the Unseen or being aided by a celestial power. This is because the indefinite noun—merit (فضل)—occurs in the context of negation, thus it implies generality.

They included the followers of Nuh (peace be upon him) and the believers among them in his call when they said: **وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا** (nor do we see you [plural] to have over us) and they did not say: "nor do we see you [singular] to have." This is because they were urging and encouraging them to follow the way they had followed.

The meaning is that your calling us—while we have what we enjoy of the advantages of worldly life such as wealth, children, knowledge, and power—would only be consistent and produce its effect if you had some merit by which you surpassed us in the adornment of worldly life, or knowledge of the Unseen, or power from the celestial realm, such that it would necessitate submission from us to you. We do not see any of that with you, so what motive necessitates our following you?

We have generalized "merit" in their speech to include merit from material aspects and others, such as knowledge of the Unseen and celestial power, contrary to most commentators who interpreted merit as material merit like wealth, numbers, and others. This is due to the generality deduced from their speech because of the occurrence of the indefinite noun in the context of negation.

Additionally, that which corresponds to this saying of theirs from the response of Nuh (peace be upon him) indicates this, which is his saying: **وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ** etc., as will follow.

And His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كاذِبِينَ** — "...rather we think you are liars." (11:27). This is a digression in the argumentation as previously alluded to. Its sum total is: "We do not see with you any matter that necessitates our following you; rather, there is a matter that necessitates not following, which is that we think you are liars."

Its meaning, according to what the context provides—and Allah knows best—is that since you do not have that with which the correctness of your call is observed, yet you persist in demanding hearing and obedience from us while you are empty-handed of the advantages of life such as wealth and prestige, this state of affairs calls for the suspicion that you are liars in your claim, desiring by it to attain what is in our hands of the aspirations of life through this means. In short, this is a sign that usually necessitates the suspicion that it is a lie used as a means to acquire wealth, seize the riches of the people, and gain superiority over them through rule and leadership. This is as Allah, the Glorified, narrated from them in a similar story when He said: **فَقالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ ما هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَتَفَضَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ** — "The elite of the faithless of his people said, ‘This is just a human being like you, who seeks to enjoy priority over you..." (23:24). By this, the reason for their linking the lie to suspicion (الظن) rather than certainty (الجزم) becomes clear, and that what is meant by the lie is the lie of the reporter (كذب مخبري) rather than the report itself.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي** — "He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord..." (11:28) to the end of the verse. This is an explanation of what Nuh (peace be upon him) answered with regarding their argument, spanning four verses. **التعمية** (obscuring) is concealment. So the meaning of "it has been obscured for you" (عميت عليكم) in the passive voice is: "it has been hidden from you due to your ignorance and your dislike for the truth." It has also been recited as *‘amiyat* in the active voice, meaning "that mercy has become hidden from you."

Since their argument was built upon the senses and the denial of what lies beyond them, and they had concluded from it first the lack of evidence for the necessity of obeying and following him, then digressed from it by progressing to the conclusion of evidence for the lack of necessity—rather, for the necessity of lack—he (peace be upon him) answered them by affirming what they attempted to negate of his messengership and what follows it, and negating what they attempted to affirm by accusing him and his followers of lying. However, he sought their sympathy with the address "O my people" (يا قوم)—adding it to the first-person pronoun—time after time to draw them to him so that his advice would find acceptance from them.

The noble verses have excelled in formulating his (peace be upon him) argument in response to them, cutting through their argument section by section and answering each section in its two aspects—meaning from the aspect of its conclusion that there is no evidence for following him (peace be upon him), and that the evidence is to the contrary. That is His saying: **يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ** etc., His saying: **وَما أَنَا بِطارِدِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** etc., and His saying: **وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ** etc. Then it took from each preceding argument something that serves as a summary, adding it to the subsequent argument and beginning with it. Thus, argument blended with argument despite the independence and completeness of each.

So the arguments were completed as three, each of them beginning with the address, which are His saying: **يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ** etc., His saying: **وَيا قَوْمِ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مالاً** etc., and His saying: **وَيا قَوْمِ مَنْ يَنْصُرُنِي مِنَ اللهِ إِنْ طَرَدْتُهُمْ** etc. So reflect upon them.

So His saying: **قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي** is a response to their saying: **ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا**, by which they meant that he has nothing but humanity in which he is similar to them and they are similar to him, so by what thing does he claim the necessity of their following him? Rather, he is a liar who wants, by what he claims of messengership, to hunt them and thereby seize their wealth and rule over them.

Since this saying of theirs implied the denial of his messengership, and their support in that was that he is a human being with no apparent trace indicating messengership and connection to the Unseen, it was necessary to alert them to that by which his truthfulness in the claim of messengership appears, which is the miraculous sign (الآية المعجزة) indicating the truthfulness of the messenger in the claim of messengership. For messengership is a type of connection to the Unseen that breaks the prevailing habit, and there is no way to know its realization except by the occurrence of another unseen matter that breaks the habit, by which it is certain that the messenger is truthful in his claim of messengership. For this reason, he (peace be upon him) alluded by his saying: **يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي** to the fact that he has a manifest proof (بينة) from Allah and a miraculous sign indicating his truthfulness in his claim.

From here it appears that what is meant by the manifest proof (البينة) is the miraculous sign that indicates the establishment of messengership, because that is what the context provides. No weight should be given to what some commentators mentioned—that what is meant by the manifest proof in the verse is the necessary knowledge (العلم الضروري) by which the Prophet knows that he is a Prophet—because it is a meaning foreign to the context.

And His saying: **وَآتانِي رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِهِ فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ** — "...and He has granted me a mercy from Himself, and it has been obscured for you..." (11:28). It appears that he (peace be upon him) is alluding by it to what Allah the Exalted gave him of the Book and knowledge. The naming of the Book, as well as the naming of knowledge of Allah and His signs, as "mercy" (رحمة) has been repeated in the Noble Quran. The Exalted said: **وَمِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتابُ مُوسى إِماماً وَرَحْمَةً** — "...and before it was the Book of Moses, a guide and a mercy..." (11:17). And He said: **وَنَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ تِبْياناً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُدىً وَرَحْمَةً** — "...and We have sent down the Book to you as an exposition of all things and as guidance and mercy..." (16:89). And He said: **فَوَجَدا عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا آتَيْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا** — "They found one of Our servants whom We had granted a mercy from Ourselves..." (18:65). And He said: **رَبَّنا لا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَيْتَنا وَهَبْ لَنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ رَحْمَةً** — "Our Lord, do not make our hearts swerve after You have guided us, and bestow Your mercy on us from Yourself..." (3:8).

As for His saying: **فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ**, it appears that its pronoun refers to the mercy. The meaning is that what I have of knowledge and gnosis has been hidden from you by your ignorance and your dislike for the truth, after I reminded you of it and spread it among you.

And His saying: **أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوها وَأَنْتُمْ لَها كارِهُونَ** — "...Shall we force it upon you while you are averse to it?" (11:28). **الإلزام** (forcing/binding) is making a thing stay with another thing such that it does not depart from it nor separate from it. What is meant by forcing the mercy upon them while they are averse to it is compelling them to believe in Allah and His signs and to be clothed in what the divine gnoses require of light and insight.

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: "Tell me, if I have a miraculous sign that confirms my messengership despite my being a human being like you, and I have what the messengership requires of a Book and knowledge that guides you to the truth, but it did not take long before your stubbornness and arrogance hid it from you—must we then compel you to it?" Meaning: "I have everything that a messenger from Allah needs in his messengership and I have made you aware of it, but you do not believe in it out of rebellion and arrogance, and it is not for me to force you to it, since there is no compulsion in the religion of Allah, the Glorified."

In the speech is an exposure of them: that the argument has been completed against them and the reality has become clear to them, yet they did not believe. However, they nevertheless want a matter for the sake of which they would believe, and it is nothing but compulsion and forcing against their will. Thus, in their saying: "We do not see you to be anything but a human being like ourselves," they desire nothing but compulsion, and there is no compulsion in the religion of Allah.

The verse is among the verses negating coercion in religion. it indicates that this is among the legislated religious rulings in the oldest of the divine laws (الشرائع), which is the law of Nuh (peace be upon him), and it remains in its validity until today without abrogation (نسخ).

It has become clear from what preceded that the verse—meaning His saying: **يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ** etc.—is a response to their saying: **ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا**. By this, the corruption of the saying of some becomes clear: that it is a response to their saying: **بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كاذِبِينَ**; and the saying of others: that it is a response to their saying: **ما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ**; and the saying of another group: that it is a response to their saying: **وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا مِنْ فَضْلٍ**. We shall not prolong the speech by addressing their clarification and refutation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيا قَوْمِ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مالاً إِنْ أَجرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ** — "O my people! I do not ask you any wealth for it. My reward lies only with Allah..." (11:29). He intends by it the response to what they accused him of regarding lying. Its necessary implication is that his call would be a way to attract their wealth and take what is in their hands out of greed for it. For if he does not ask them for any of their wealth, they have no right to accuse him of that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما أَنَا بِطارِدِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا رَبِّهِمْ وَلكِنِّي أَراكُمْ قَوْماً تَجْهَلُونَ** — "...and I will not drive away those who have faith. They will indeed encounter their Lord. But I see that you are an ignorant people." (11:29). This is a response to their saying: **وَما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ**. He replaced the word "the lowest" (الأراذل)—which is a word of disparagement and belittlement—with His saying: "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) out of veneration for the matter of their faith and as an indication of their connection to their Lord.

He negated in his response that he would drive them away and justified that by His saying: **إِنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا رَبِّهِمْ** (they will indeed encounter their Lord), as an announcement that they have a day in which they will return to Allah, so He will call them to account for their deeds and recompense them for what they have done of good or evil. Thus, their account is with their Lord and no one else has any authority. It is not for Nuh (peace be upon him) to call them to account and recompense them with anything. But the people, due to their ignorance, expect the poor, the indigent, and the weak to be driven away from the society of good and to be stripped of blessing, nobility, and dignity.

It has become clear that what is meant by His saying: **إِنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا رَبِّهِمْ** is an allusion to the accounting of Allah, the Glorified, for them on the day they return to Him and encounter Him, as occurred in the like of this meaning in His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ ما عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَما مِنْ حِسابِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَتَطْرُدَهُمْ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** — "Do not drive away those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening, desiring His face. Their account is not your responsibility at all, and your account is not their responsibility at all, that you should drive them away and thus become one of the wrongdoers." (6:52).

As for the saying of one who said: the meaning of His saying: **إِنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا رَبِّهِمْ** is that he does not drive them away because they will encounter their Lord, so He will recompense whoever wronged them and drove them away; or that they will encounter the reward of their Lord, so how can they be the lowest and how can it be permissible to drive them away when they do not deserve that—this is far from the understanding. Moreover, the first of the two meanings makes the following verse—meaning His saying: **وَيا قَوْمِ مَنْ يَنْصُرُنِي مِنَ اللهِ إِنْ طَرَدْتُهُمْ**—redundant and unnecessary, as is apparent.

It has also become clear that what is meant by His saying: **وَلكِنِّي أَراكُمْ قَوْماً تَجْهَلُونَ** is their ignorance of the matter of the Return (المعاد) and that the account and recompense are for Allah, not for anyone else. As for what some mentioned—that what is meant by it is the ignorance that is the opposite of intellect and forbearance, meaning "you act foolishly toward them"; or the meaning that you are ignorant that the reality of distinction between one human and another is by following the truth, performing righteous deeds, and being adorned with virtues, not by wealth and prestige as you think—this is a meaning far from the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيا قَوْمِ مَنْ يَنْصُرُنِي مِنَ اللهِ إِنْ طَرَدْتُهُمْ أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ** — "O my people! Who will help me against Allah if I drive them away? Will you not then take admonition?" (11:30). **النصر** (help) here is imbued with the meaning of prevention or salvation and the like. The meaning is: "Who will prevent me or who will save me from the punishment of Allah if I drive them away? Will you not remember that it is an injustice, and Allah, the Glorified, helps the wronged against the wrongdoer and takes vengeance upon him?" The intellect is certain that Allah, the Glorified, does not equate the wrongdoer and the wronged, nor does He leave the wrongdoer to wrong without recompensing him for his wrongdoing with that which grieves him and heals the burning of the wronged one's chest. And Allah is All-Mighty, Possessor of Vengeance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ** — "I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say that I am an angel..." (11:31). This is a response to their saying: **وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا مِنْ فَضْلٍ**. He returns their saying to them: "I do not claim any of the merit that you expect me to claim by virtue of my claiming messengership." For you imagine that the messenger must possess the treasuries of divine mercy, thus becoming independent in enriching the poor, healing the sick, reviving the dead, and disposing of the heavens, the earth, and the rest of the parts of the cosmos as he wishes and how he wishes.

And that he must possess knowledge of the Unseen, thus obtaining every good hidden from the eyes and veiled from the sights, drawing it to himself, and repelling every future evil latent from himself; in short, increasing in goods and being protected from dislikes.

And that he must rise from the degree of humanity to the station of angelhood—meaning to be an angel (ملك) purified from the pollutions of nature and free from the needs and deficiencies of humanity, such that he does not eat, nor drink, nor marry, nor fall into the toil of earning a livelihood and acquiring the necessities and goods of life.

These are the aspects of merit that you imagine the messenger must be given and possess, thus becoming independent in them. You have erred; for the messenger has nothing but the message, and I do not claim any of that. So I do not say to you that I have the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say that I am an angel. In short, I do not claim any of the merit that you expect such that you would belie me for its absence. I only say that I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord that confirms my messengership, and He has granted me a mercy from Himself.

What is meant by His saying: **خَزائِنُ اللهِ** (the treasuries of Allah) is all the unseen stores and treasures from which creatures are provided with what they need for their existence and survival, and by which they seek help in completing and perfecting their deficiencies.

Those are the ones that the common folk imagine the Prophets and the Friends of Allah (الأولياء) are given the keys to, and by which they possess the power to do what they wish and decree what they want, as was proposed to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) and Allah the Exalted narrated it when He says: **وَقالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى تَفْجُرَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ يَنْبُوعاً أَوْ تَكُونَ لَكَ جَنَّةٌ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَعِنَبٍ فَتُفَجِّرَ الْأَنْهارَ خِلالَها تَفْجِيراً أَوْ تُسْقِطَ السَّماءَ كَما زَعَمْتَ عَلَيْنا كِسَفاً أَوْ تَأْتِيَ بِاللهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةِ قَبِيلاً أَوْ يَكُونَ لَكَ بَيْتٌ مِنْ زُخْرُفٍ أَوْ تَرْقى فِي السَّماءِ وَلَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولاً** — "They say, ‘We will not believe you until you make a spring gush forth from the ground for us, or until you have a garden of date palms and vines and you make rivers gush through them, or until you make the sky fall upon us in pieces as you claim, or until you bring Allah and the angels face to face [with us], or until you have a house of gold, or until you ascend into the sky. And we will not believe your ascent until you bring down for us a book that we may read.’ Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human messenger?’" (17:90-93).

He only said: **وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ** (nor do I know the Unseen) and did not say: "nor do I say that I know the Unseen," because this type of knowledge, since it was something that is begrudged and not permitted to be shown, the saying of a speaker "I do not say that I know the Unseen" would not negate its existence with the speaker. Rather, it needs to be said: "I do not know the Unseen" to provide the negation. This is in contrast to his saying: **لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ** and his saying: **وَلا أَقُولُ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ**. He did not repeat his saying: **لَكُمْ** (to you) because one occurrence was sufficient.

Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) to address his people with what Nuh (peace be upon him) addressed his people, then He appended it with that which makes the intent clear when He said: **قُلْ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَّ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ أَفَلا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ** — "Say, ‘I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say to you that I am an angel. I follow only what is revealed to me.’ Say, ‘Are the blind and the seeing equal? Will you not then reflect?’" (6:50).

Look at His saying: **لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ** etc., then at His saying: **إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَ**, then at His saying: **قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ** etc. He first negates the merit that the common people expect from their Prophet, then He affirms for the messenger the message only. Then He hastens to affirm merit from another aspect, other than the aspect that the people expect, which is that he is seeing by the sight given by Allah the Exalted, and that others in relation to him are like the blind in relation to the seeing. This is the motive for their following him, just as the blind follows the seeing, and it is what permits him to call them to follow him.

## (A Philosophical and Quranic Discourse on the Power of the Prophets and the Friends of Allah)

People are in ignorance of the station of their Lord and in heedlessness of the meaning of His encompassment and dominance. Despite what the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — guides them to of His existence and His oneness, the trial of the world of matter and nature, the immersion in natural laws and rules, then the social customs and laws, and the familiarity with multiplicity and separation, leads them to measure the Divine Realm by what they are familiar with in the world of matter. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is to them, along with His creation, like a tyrant among the tyrants of humanity with his slaves and subjects.

There is an individual among humans whom we call, for example, a king or a tyrant, beneath whom are ministers, princes, soldiers, and henchmen who carry out what he commands or forbids. He has gifts and boons for whom he wishes, and will and dislike, taking and returning, seizing and releasing, mercy and wrath, decree and abrogation, and so on.

Each of the king, his servants, his working hands, his subjects, and what is in their hands of blessings and the goods of life, is an existing, limited, independent entity (موجود محدود مستقل الوجود), separated from others. They are only linked to one another by conventional rules, laws, and customs that have no place except the mind of the thinker and the belief of the believer.

They have applied the Divine Realm—meaning what prophecy informs of the station of the Lord, the Exalted, His attributes, His acts, His angels, His books, and His messengers—to this system. Thus, He, the Exalted, wills and dislikes, gives and withholds, and manages the system of creation just as one of us called a king does. He is limited in existence, isolated in being; and each of His angels and the rest of His creation is independent in existence, possessing what they have of existence and bestowed blessings apart from Allah, the Glorified. And Allah was in the pre-eternity of time (أزل الزمان) alone, with nothing of His creation with Him, then He originated the creation in the side of post-eternity, so they were with Him.

They have affirmed—as you see—a limited existent whose existence is coincident with time, except that His temporal existence is perpetual. He has power over everything, knowledge of everything, a will that is not broken, and a decree that is not returned. He is independent in what He has of attributes and acts, just as one of us is independent, thus possessing what he has of life, knowledge, power, and so on. So his life is a life for him and is not for Allah; his knowledge is his knowledge, not the knowledge of Allah; his power is his power, not the power of Allah, and so on. It is only said of our existence, or our life, or our knowledge, or our power that they are for Allah just as it is said of what the subjects have of blessing that it is for the king, in the sense that it was with him, then he took it out from himself and placed it with us for us to dispose of. All of this—as you see—stands on the basis of limitation and isolation.

However, certain proofs (البراهين اليقينية) decree the corruption of all of that. For they judge the permeation of poverty and need into the possible existents (الموجودات الممكنة) in their essences and the effects of their essences. If the need for Him, the Exalted, is in the station of the essence (الذات), it is impossible to be independent of Him or isolated from Him at all. For if independence were assumed for a thing from Him, the Exalted, in its existence or any of the effects of its existence—in whatever way assumed, in origination or survival—it would be independent of Him from that aspect, which is impossible.

So every possible being (ممكن) is non-independent in anything of its essence and the effects of its essence. And Allah, the Glorified, is the One Who is independent in His essence, and He is the Rich (الغني) Who does not lack in anything, nor does He lose anything of existence and the perfection of existence such as life, power, and knowledge; thus He has no limit by which He is limited. Some clarification of this issue has already preceded in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: **لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ ثالِثُ ثَلاثَةٍ** — "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity’..." (5:73).

Based on what has preceded, what the possible being has of existence, life, power, or knowledge is dependent in its existence upon Him, the Exalted, and is not independent of Him in any way. There is no difference in that between the little and the much, as long as the characteristic of non-independence is preserved in it. Thus, there is no barrier to assuming a possible being that has knowledge of everything, or power over everything, or perpetual life, as long as it is non-independent in existence from Allah, the Glorified, nor isolated in being from Him; just as there is no barrier to the realization of a possible being with a temporary existence having a term, or knowledge or power related to some things and not others. Yes, the assumption of independence (الاستقلال) nullifies the possible need, and there is no difference in it between the much and the little, as you have known. This is from the aspect of the intellect.

As for the aspect of transmission (النقل), the Divine Book, although it speaks of the exclusivity of some attributes and acts to Him, the Exalted—such as knowledge of the hidden things, reviving, causing death, and creating, as in His saying: **وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ** — "With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him..." (6:59), and His saying: **وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ أَماتَ وَأَحْيا** — "...and that it is He who causes death and gives life..." (53:44), and His saying: **اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها** — "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death..." (39:42), and His saying: **اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "Allah is the creator of all things..." (39:62), and other verses—yet they are all interpreted by other verses, such as His saying: **عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ** — "[He is] the Knower of the Unseen, and He does not expose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves..." (72:26-27), and His saying: **قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ** — "Say, ‘The angel of death... will take your souls...’" (32:11), and His saying regarding ‘Isa (peace be upon him): **وَأُحْيِ الْمَوْتى بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** — "...and I revive the dead by Allah’s leave..." (3:49), and His saying: **وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِي فَتَنْفُخُ فِيها فَتَكُونُ طَيْراً بِإِذْنِي** — "...and when you would fashion from clay the form of a bird by My leave, and you would breathe into it and it would become a bird by My leave..." (5:110), and other verses.

The joining of verses to verses leaves no doubt that what is meant by the negating verses is the exclusivity of these matters to Him, the Exalted, by way of originality and independence (الأصالة والاستقلال); and what is meant by the affirming verses is the possibility of their realization in other than Him, the Exalted, by way of following and non-independence.

So whoever affirms something of the hidden knowledge or the unseen power—meaning knowledge not through the path of thought and power not through its usual natural course—for other than Him, the Exalted, from His Prophets and Friends, as has occurred frequently in the reports and traditions, and negates with it originality and independence—such that the knowledge and power, for example, are for Him, the Exalted, and what appeared from him only appeared through mediation and what occurred from him only occurred by His bestowing existence—then there is no restriction upon him.

But whoever affirms something of that by way of originality and independence, according to what the common understanding affirms, even if he ascribes it to Allah, the Glorified, and the outpouring of His mercy, has not escaped from exaggeration (الغلو) and was included in the like of His saying: **لا تَغْلُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ وَلا تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَّ** — "...Do not exceed the bounds in your religion, and do not attribute to Allah anything but the truth..." (4:171).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ خَيْراً اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ إِنِّي إِذاً لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** — "...nor do I say of those whom your eyes despise that Allah will never grant them any good—Allah knows best what is in their souls—for then I would indeed be one of the wrongdoers.’" (11:31). It is said in *al-Mufradat*: **زريت** (I disparaged) him means I found fault with him; and *azraytu bihi* means I belittled him, and likewise *izdaraytu bihi*, and its root is *ifta‘altu*. He said: "whom your eyes despise" (تزدري أعينكم) means they deem them insignificant. Its estimation is: "their eyes despise them," meaning they deem them insignificant and hold them in contempt. [End of quote].

This section of his speech (peace be upon him) is an allusion to what the elite who disbelieved from his people believed and upon which they built the custom of nobility and the way of lordship. It is that the individuals of humanity are divided into two types: the strong and the weak. As for the strong, they are the possessors of wealth and the masters of power, supported by money and numbers. As for the weak, they are the rest. The strong are the masters in human society; for them is the blessing and the dignity, and for their sake society is formed. Others from the weak are created for their sake, intended for them, sacrifices for their interests, like the subjects in relation to the throne of an absolute government, the slaves in relation to the masters, the servants and workers in relation to the served, and women in relation to men; and ultimately, every weak one in relation to the strong one who is superior over him.

In short, their belief was that the weak one in society is a degraded human or an animal in the form of a human, who only enters into society and participates with them in life so that the noble may benefit from his work and profit from the toil of his right hand for his life, without the reverse. Rather, he is deprived of dignity, driven away from the enclosure of nobility, and despairing of mercy and care.

This is what they used to see, and it was what was relied upon in their society. Nuh (peace be upon him) refuted that to them by his saying: **وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ خَيْراً**.

Then he explained their error in their belief by His saying: **اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ** (Allah knows best what is in their souls). Meaning: "Your eyes only despise them, belittle them, and hold their affair in contempt because they perceive the outward appearance of their weakness and insignificance. But that is not the criterion for attaining good and reaching dignity. Rather, the criterion in that—and especially the divine dignities and rewards—is the matter of the soul and its being adorned with the ornaments of virtue and spiritual merit. There is no way for me nor for you to the knowledge of the inner depths of the souls and the secrets of the hearts except for Allah, the Glorified. So it is not for me nor for you to judge their deprivation of good and felicity."

Then he explained by His saying: **إِنِّي إِذاً لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** (for then I would indeed be one of the wrongdoers) the reason for his recoiling from this saying. Its meaning is that it is a saying without knowledge, and the prohibition of good to one who may deserve it, arbitrarily without evidence, is an injustice (ظلم) that a person should not intend, thereby entering into the ranks of the wrongdoers.

This meaning is what the Exalted alludes to in what He narrates of the speech of the People of the Elevations (أصحاب الأعراف) on the Day of Resurrection, addressing these tyrants, when He says: **وَنادى أَصْحابُ الْأَعْرافِ رِجالاً يَعْرِفُونَهُمْ بِسِيماهُمْ قالُوا ما أَغْنى عَنْكُمْ جَمْعُكُمْ وَما كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ أَهؤُلاءِ الَّذِينَ أَقْسَمْتُمْ لا يَنالُهُمُ اللهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ** — "The occupants of the Elevations will call out to certain men whom they recognize by their marks, saying, ‘Your gathering did not avail you, nor what you used to be arrogant about. Are these the ones of whom you swore that Allah would never grant them any mercy?’" (7:48-49).

In the speech—meaning the saying of Nuh (peace be upon him): **وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ** etc.—is an exposure of them: that just as they used to prohibit the weak of society from the social advantages of life, they likewise used to prohibit them from religious dignity and say: "They will not be felicitous through religion; rather, only the nobles of society and its strong ones will be felicitous by it." In it is also an exposure that they are wrongdoers.

Nuh (peace be upon him) only followed his saying: **وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ**—in which he negates the aspects of distinction that they expected in the messenger from himself—with his saying: **وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ خَيْراً** etc., even though it refers to the weak who believed in him from his people, because the elite had linked them to him in their saying: **وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا مِنْ فَضْلٍ**.

Its clarification is that the meaning of this saying of theirs is: "Our following you and those who believed in you of these lowest ones would only be consistent due to a merit that is established for you over us, and we do not see you to have any merit over us. As for you, you do not have what is exclusive to the messenger of celestial power or knowledge of the Unseen, or that you be an angel purified from the pollutions of matter and nature. As for those who believe in you, they are only our lowest ones who are despairing of human dignity, deprived of mercy and care."

So Nuh answered them with what means: "As for me, I do not claim anything of what you expect from my messengership; for the messenger has nothing but the message. As for these weak ones who have insignificance with you, it is possible that Allah knows good from their souls, so He gives them good and merit; for He is most knowing of their souls. The criterion for religious dignity and divine mercy is the purity of the soul and the soundness of the heart, not the outward appearance that your eyes despise. So I do not say: 'Allah will never grant them any good,' for it is an injustice that would enter me into the ranks of the wrongdoers."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا يا نُوحُ قَدْ جادَلْتَنا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدالَنا فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ** — "They said, ‘O Noah, you have disputed with us and you have disputed with us much. Now bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful.’" (11:32). This is speech they delivered to Nuh (peace be upon him) after they were unable to refute his argument and nullify the truth he called to. it is formulated in the manner of challenging (التعجيز). What is meant by their saying: **بِما تَعِدُنا** (with what you threaten us) is what he warned them of at the beginning of his call of the punishment of a painful day.

Allah, the Glorified, has presented this saying of theirs as a separate section without branching (تفريع), because they only said it after he had remained among them for a long period calling them to monotheism, contending with them, and arguing with them with various types of contention and argumentation until he cut off all their excuses and made the truth clear to them, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, in what He narrates from him (peace be upon him) in his supplication: **قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُ قَوْمِي لَيْلاً وَنَهاراً**—until he said—**ثُمَّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُهُمْ جِهاراً ثُمَّ إِنِّي أَعْلَنْتُ لَهُمْ وَأَسْرَرْتُ لَهُمْ إِسْراراً** — "He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed I have called my people night and day... Then I called them aloud. Then I spoke to them publicly and confided with them privately." (71:5-9). And in Surah al-Ankabut: **فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عاماً** — "...and he remained among them for a thousand years less fifty years..." (29:14). So this which Allah has presented of his argumentation with his people and their response in the form of a single dialogue actually occurred over hundreds of years. This has many parallels in the Noble Quran and there is nothing strange in it; for the One Who narrates that is Allah, the Glorified, Who encompasses time and everything in it, and the one who hears it through revelation is the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), who has been given such breadth of vision that the various nations and the ends of time gather with him.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: "O Nuh, you have disputed with us and increased your disputation with us until we have become weary and bored, and we are not going to believe in you. So bring us what you threaten us with of the punishment." They do not admit to the inability to contend and dispute with him; rather, they make him despair of themselves in argumentation and ask him to occupy himself with what the caller who despairs of hearing and obedience occupies himself with, which is the evil with which he threatens them and mentions alongside his advice.

His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ إِن شَاءَ وَمَا أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ** "He said, ‘Allah will bring it to you only if He wishes, and you cannot frustrate [Him].’" (11:33) Since their saying: **فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَا** "so bring us what you threaten us with" (11:32) etc., was a demand for him to bring the punishment upon them—and that is not within his power, for he is but a messenger—he responded to this suggestion of theirs as well. In the context of a "reversal restriction" (قصر القلب), he clarified that bringing the punishment is not up to him; rather, it is solely up to Allah. He is the One who owns your affair, so He will bring you the punishment I promised you by His command. He is your Lord, and to Him the return of your entire affair belongs. Nothing of the administrative governance (التدبير) returns to me, such that my promising you the punishment or your suggestion to me to demand it would affect anything in the presence of His grandeur. If He wishes, He will bring it to you, and if He does not wish, He will not.

From here, it becomes clear that his saying (peace be upon him): **إِن شَاءَ** "if He wishes" is among the most subtle conditions in this context. Through it, the right of "transcendental sanctification" (التنزيه) — *the recognition of Allah’s absolute freedom from any external compulsion or limitation* — was conveyed. This means that nothing governs Allah the Glorified, nor does any subduer overpower Him; He does what He wills, and none other than He does what He wills. This is similar to what will come at the end of the Surah regarding the exception in His saying: **خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ رَبُّكَ ۖ عَطَاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ** "to remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty." (11:108)

And his saying: **وَمَا أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ** "and you cannot frustrate [Him]" is another sanctification of Allah the Glorified. At the same time, it is a response to the "defiant challenge" (الأمر التعجيزي) they threw at him (peace be upon him). Its outward appearance suggested they paid no heed to the punishment he threatened them with, as if they were capable of frustrating Him (معجزون) and could not be overcome.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي إِنْ أَرَدْتُ أَنْ أَنصَحَ لَكُمْ إِن كَانَ اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ** "My advice will not benefit you, were I to advise you, if Allah should desire to cause you to perish." (11:34) etc. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Advice (النصح) is the pursuit of an action or word in which there is the well-being (صلاح) of its recipient." He said: "It is from their saying: 'I advised (نصحت) him in affection,' meaning I was sincere (أخلصته) to him. Pure honey is called *nasih* (ناصح). Or it is from their saying: 'I advised (نصحت) the skin,' meaning I sewed it; the tailor is called *nasih* (ناصح) and the thread *nisah* (نصاح)."

He also said: "Error (الغي) is ignorance (جهل) arising from a corrupt belief (اعتقاد فاسد). This is because ignorance may sometimes be a person having no belief—neither sound nor corrupt—and sometimes it arises from believing in something corrupt. This second type is called *ghayy* (غي). The Exalted said: **مَا ضَلَّ صَاحِبُكُمْ وَمَا غَوَىٰ** 'Your companion has neither gone astray, nor is he gone into error' (53:2), and He said: **وَإِخْوَانُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِي الْغَيِّ** 'But their brethren, they draw them into error' (7:202)." End quote.

Based on this, the difference between "leading into error" (الإغواء) and "misguiding" (الإضلال) is that misguiding (الإضلال) — *the act of leading someone away from the correct path* — is a removal from the path while the destination remains present in the mind of the one straying. Leading into error (الإغواء) — *the act of causing one to lose their way through ignorance or corruption of belief* — is a removal from the path while the destination itself vanishes from his mind due to his preoccupation with something else out of ignorance.

Will (الإرادة) and volition (المشية) are nearly synonymous. From Allah the Glorified, they refer to the "causation of causes" (تسبيب الأسباب) that lead to the existence of a thing by necessity. Thus, a thing being "willed" by Him, the Exalted, means He has completed and perfected the causes of its existence; therefore, it exists inevitably. As for the essence of the ongoing "causality" (السببية), it is willed by itself. Hence it is said: "Allah created things by volition (المشية), and the volition by itself."

In short, his saying: **وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي** "My advice will not benefit you" etc., is like one side of a "disjunctive dilemma" (الترديد), and the other side is his saying: **وَمَا أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ** "and you cannot frustrate [Him]." It is as if he (peace be upon him) is saying: Your affair is with Allah; if He wishes to punish you, He will bring the punishment upon you, and nothing can repel His punishment or overpower His volition. Thus, you cannot frustrate Him, nor will my advice benefit you—were I to desire to advise you—once Allah has willed to lead you into error so that you disbelieve in Him, and the word of punishment becomes due against you. He conditioned his advice with a "stipulation" (الشرط) because they did not concede to him that he was indeed advising them.

Leading into error (الإغواء), like misguiding (الإضلال), is not permissible to attribute to Him, the Exalted, if it is "initial" (ابتدائي). However, it is permissible if it is by way of "requital" (المجازاة). For instance, when a person sins and deserves error thereby, Allah denies him the "means of success" (أسباب التوفيق) and leaves him to his own soul (يخليه ونفسه); thus, he falls into error and strays from the path of truth. The Exalted said: **يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفَاسِقِينَ** "He misleads many thereby, and He guides many thereby; and He misleads no one thereby except the transgressors" (2:26).

In the discourse, there is an indication that the descent of the "punishment of extirpation" (عذاب الاستئصال) upon them is preceded by divine "leading into error" (الإغواء), as alluded to in His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذَا أَرَدْنَا أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا فِيهَا فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا الْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَاهَا تَدْمِيراً** "When We desire to destroy a town, We command its affluent ones [to obey Us]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly" (17:16). And He said: **وَقَيَّضْنَا لَهُمْ قُرَنَاءَ فَزَيَّنُوا لَهُم مَّا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ** "We assigned them companions who glosssed over for them what was before them and what was behind them. So the word became due against them" (41:25).

His saying: **هُوَ رَبُّكُمْ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ** "He is your Lord, and to Him you will be brought back" (11:34) is a "justification" (تعليل) for His saying: **وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي** "My advice will not benefit you" etc., or for His saying: **إِنَّمَا يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ إِن شَاءَ** "Allah will bring it to you only if He wishes" up to His saying: **يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ** "desire to cause you to perish" altogether. The sum of it is that the matter of "governing the servants" (تدبير العباد) belongs to the Lord to Whom all matters return. Allah the Glorified is your Lord and to Him you return; thus, it is not for me to bring you a promised punishment, nor is it for you to frustrate Him if He wishes to bring the punishment upon you—and so He brings it to extirpate you—nor is it for my advice to benefit you if He wills to lead you into error in order to punish you.

Regarding His saying: **إِن كَانَ اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ** "if Allah should desire to cause you to perish," they have mentioned several modes of "interpretation" (التأويل):

**First:** That the meaning is "He punishes you for your disbelief." Allah the Exalted has called punishment *ghayy* (غياً) in His saying: **فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا** "so they will soon face [the rewards of their] straying" (19:59).

**Second:** That the intended meaning is: "If Allah desires the punishment for your leading the creation into error (إغوائكم) and your misguiding them." It is the custom of the Arabs to name the punishment by the name of the thing being punished. Of this category is His saying: **اللَّهُ يَسْتَهْزِئُ بِهِمْ** "Allah shall deride them" (2:15), meaning He punishes them for their derision; and His saying: **وَمَكَرُوا وَمَكَرَ اللَّهُ** "And they plotted, and Allah devised" (3:54), meaning He punished them for their plotting, and so on.

**Third:** That leading into error (الإغواء) is in the sense of "destruction" (الإهلاك). Thus, the meaning is "He desires to destroy you." This is from their saying: "The young camel *ghawiya* (غوي)," when it perishes from drinking too much milk.

**Fourth:** That the people of Noah used to believe that Allah the Exalted misguides His servants from the religion, and that what they were upon was by the will of Allah; had it not been so, He would have changed it and compelled them to the contrary. So Noah said to them, by way of "amazement" (تعجب) at their statement and "denial" (إنكار) of it, that "my advice will not benefit you if the matter is as you say."

By reflecting on what we have previously presented, you will realize that the discourse is in no need of these interpretations.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ ۖ قُلْ إِنِ افْتَرَيْتُهُ فَعَلَيَّ إِجْرَامِي وَأَنَا بَرِيءٌ مِّمَّا تُجْرِمُونَ** "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’? Say, ‘If I have fabricated it, my guilt will be upon me, and I am quit of your guilt.’" (11:35). The root of "guilt" (الجرم) — as mentioned by al-Raghib in his *Mufradat* — is the cutting of fruit from the tree. *Ajrama* (أجرم) means he became a possessor of *jurm* (جرم). It was then used metaphorically for every "disliked acquisition" (اكتساب مكروه). Thus, *al-jurm* (الجرم), with either a *damma* or *fatha* on the *jim*, means a disliked acquisition, which is "sin" (المعصية).

The verse occurs as a "parenthetical interjection" (اعتراض). The "subtle point" (النكتة) in it is that the call of Noah and his "arguments" (احتجاجات) against the idolatry of his people—especially what Allah the Exalted has cited in this Surah of his argumentation—is the thing most similar to the call of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his argumentation against the idolatry of his nation.

If you wish for further confirmation of this, return to Surah al-An'am—which is in reality the Surah of Argumentation—and compare what Allah the Exalted narrated from Noah in this Surah with what Allah commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in that Surah in His saying: **قُل لَّا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِندِي خَزَائِنُ اللَّهِ وَلَا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلَا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ** "Say, ‘I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say to you that I am an angel’" (6:50) until He said: **وَلَا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُم بِالْغَدَاةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ** "And do not drive away those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening" (6:52) until He said: **قُل لَّا أَتَّبِعُ أَهْوَاءَكُمْ ۙ قَدْ ضَلَلْتُ إِذًا وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُهْتَدِينَ قُلْ إِنِّي عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّي وَكَذَّبْتُم بِهِ** "Say, ‘I do not follow your desires, for then I would have gone astray, and I would not be among the rightly guided.’ Say, ‘Indeed I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and you have denied it.’" (6:56-57).

You may also apply the rest of what was mentioned of his arguments (peace be upon him) in Surah Nuh and al-A'raf to what was mentioned of arguments in Surah al-An'am and in this Surah; you will then witness the truth of what we have claimed.

Due to this similarity and "correspondence" (المناسبة), it was appropriate to transition—after mentioning the arguments of Noah (peace be upon him) in his warning to his people by the command of Allah the Glorified—to what they accused the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of and the "fabrication" (الافتراء) against Allah they charged him with. Yet he does not warn them nor present arguments to them except as Noah (peace be upon him) warned and presented arguments to his people. This is like a king's messenger who warns his people and the rebels who disdain obedience, offering them advice and "completing the proof" (إتمام الحجة) against them, only for them to charge him with being a fabricator against the king and that there is no duty of obedience. He returns to them with advice a second time, mentioning the story of another sincere messenger from the king to another people whom he advised just as he advised them, but they did not gain insight and thus perished. Whenever he mentions his arguments and "exhortations" (مواعظ) to them, "intense emotion" (الوجد) and regret move him to remember their charge of fabrication against him. He regrets this, saying: You charge me with fabrication, yet I have mentioned to you nothing but what this messenger spread among his people of the word of wisdom and advice. Certainly, if I have fabricated it, then my guilt is upon me; do not accept my word, but I am quit of your deeds.

He, the Glorified, returned to the command for such a "confrontation" (المباراة) a second time at the end of the Surah after citing several stories of the messengers, where He said: **وَكُلًّا نَّقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الرُّسُلِ مَا نُثَبِّتُ بِهِ فُؤَادَكَ** "And We relate to you every significant saga of the apostles with which We strengthen your heart" (11:120) until He said: **وَقُل لِّلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ اعْمَلُوا عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنَّا عَامِلُونَ وَانتَظِرُوا إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ** "And say to those who do not believe, ‘Act according to your ability; we too are acting. And wait! We too are waiting.’" (11:121-122).

Some commentators mentioned that the verse is part of the completion of the story and the address in it is to Noah. The meaning would be: Do the people of Noah say Noah fabricated it? Say, O Noah, if I have fabricated it, then my guilt is upon me and I am quit of the guilt you commit. According to this, the discourse contains a type of "shift" (التفات) from the third person to the second person, but this is extremely far from the "context" (سياق) of the speech.

In His saying: **وَأَنَا بَرِيءٌ مِّمَّا تُجْرِمُونَ** "and I am quit of your guilt," there is an affirmation of a "continuous guilt" (إجرام مستمر) on their part, which has been presented as a "foregone conclusion" (إرسال المسلمات), just as in His saying: **فَعَلَيَّ إِجْرَامِي** "my guilt will be upon me" there is an affirmation of guilt. This is because what was mentioned of Noah's arguments, if it were fabrication, would be a lie in the sense that Noah (peace be upon him) did not use these arguments—even though they are true. However, insofar as they are "decisive rational arguments" (حجج عقلية قاطعة) that do not admit of falsehood, they establish for these disbelievers a continuous guilt in rejecting what he guides them to of faith and righteous deeds. Thus, in their departure from the requirement of these arguments, they are definitely guilty. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is guilty not definitely, but on the "supposition" (تقدير) that he is a fabricator—and he is not a fabricator.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Ibn Abi Nasr al-Bazanti, from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), he said: Allah said regarding Noah (peace be upon him): **وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي إِنْ أَرَدْتُ أَنْ أَنصَحَ لَكُمْ إِن كَانَ اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ** "My advice will not benefit you, were I to advise you, if Allah should desire to cause you to perish." He said: "The matter is up to Allah—He guides and He misleads."

**I say:** Its explanation has already passed.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ** "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’?" the verse: al-Shaybani in *Nahj al-Bayan* narrated from Muqatil, who said: "The disbelievers of Mecca said: 'Indeed Muhammad fabricated the Quran.'" He said: "The like of that has been narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them)."
